summary: The post argues that Silicon Valley’s love of the “pivot” reveals a deeper truth: startups are less about specific missions and more about ambition, status, and building anything people want. That same social scoreboard shapes what counts as impact, which is why attention is now drifting from software to Washington—another pivot that shows achievement is relative and socially constructed.
money_quotes: Because, at their most honest core, startups don’t exist to solve a specific problem; they exist to make something, anything, that people want.
These things are instead circumstantial vehicles of ambition, the disposable boosters of a hopeful rocket ship.
That’s why we built 140 characters instead of flying cars, and recreated our parents as apps: Because you can build a company on those ideas, and building a company is what we celebrate.
A spoonful of startup sugar helps the civic medicine go down.
key_insight: Silicon Valley’s pivots and shifting obsessions show that achievement is less about fixed missions than about socially rewarded status, which can move from startups to policy on a whim.
